title: Gender and the Attention Economy
thesis: Women in India face a double bind in the attention economy: lower access and digital skills limit participation, while those who do participate encounter disproportionate care burdens, safety risks, and economic inequality.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Women in India face a double bind in the attention economy: lower access and digital skills limit participation, while those who do participate encounter disproportionate care burdens, safety risks, and economic inequality.

evidence.snippet: NSSO CAMS 2022-23 finds roughly eight in ten women had recently used a mobile phone versus over nine in ten men, and roughly half of women had recently used the internet versus about two-thirds of men.
NSSO Time Use Survey 2024 finds women spent 289 minutes/day on unpaid domestic services and 137 minutes/day on caregiving, far above men's 88 and 75 minutes respectively.
Parliamentary committee report summarised by PRS notes cybercrimes against women rose 239% between 2017 and 2022.

counterevidence.summary: The gap is narrowing: women now make up 47% of India's internet users, the highest share on record, and the gender gap nearly vanishes among children.

claims.claim: NSSO CAMS 2022-23 data show a sharp gender gap at the point of digital entry: roughly eight in ten women had recently used a mobile phone, compared with over nine in ten men, and roughly half of women had recently used the internet, compared with about two-thirds of men.

evidence.snippet: About 85% of adults used a mobile phone in the last three months; about eight in ten women had used phones versus over nine in ten men. Two-thirds of men had used the internet versus half of women.

counterevidence.summary: Phone and internet use are self-reported and measured over a three-month window; some women may use devices occasionally without considering themselves users.

claims.claim: GSMA's Mobile Gender Gap Report 2024 shows that women in low- and middle-income countries are about 15% less likely than men to use mobile internet, with roughly 60% of unconnected women concentrated in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa; India-specific estimates show women are 33% less likely than men to use mobile internet.

evidence.snippet: Women in LMICs are 15% less likely than men to use mobile internet; around 60% of unconnected women live in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa.
Indian women are 33% less likely than men to use mobile internet, and only about 30% know how to use the internet to find information, operate mobile money, or access apps and e-services.

counterevidence.summary: The 15% figure is a global LMIC estimate, not India-specific; India's actual gap may differ. The 33% India figure comes from GSMA data cited in a policy synthesis.

claims.claim: NSSO Time Use Survey 2024 found that women who performed unpaid domestic services spent 289 minutes a day on them, compared with 88 minutes for men, and women spent 137 minutes on caregiving versus men's 75 minutes.

evidence.snippet: Females spent 289 minutes/day on unpaid domestic services; males spent 88 minutes. Females spent 137 minutes/day on caregiving; males spent 75 minutes.

counterevidence.summary: Figures are averages among those who performed the activity, not the whole population; they do not capture seasonal variation or differences by age, class, or region.

claims.claim: Women's leisure and mass-media time is more fragmented by unpaid care work than men's, so the attention economy extracts a scarcer, more interrupted resource from women even when raw minutes look similar.

evidence.snippet: Men spent 177 minutes/day and women 164 minutes/day on culture, leisure, and mass-media use among participants.

counterevidence.summary: The NSSO TUS does not measure fragmentation or interruption directly; this claim is an inference from the large gender gap in unpaid work and caregiving.

claims.claim: A 2026 parliamentary committee report summarised by PRS Legislative Research noted that cybercrimes against women in India rose 239% between 2017 and 2022.

evidence.snippet: As per National Crime Record Bureau data, between 2017 and 2022 there has been a 239% increase in cybercrimes against women.

counterevidence.summary: Reported cybercrime counts reflect both real incidence and reporting willingness, police capacity, and legal classification; actual rates are likely undercounted.

claims.claim: The creator economy and high-skill digital work remain unevenly accessible to women: only about 2.2% of employed Indian women work in business services, partly due to the digital-skills gap.

evidence.snippet: Only 2.2% of employed women in India work in business services, and women's lower digital skills and access constrain movement into high-productivity ICT services.

counterevidence.summary: Business-services employment is shaped by education, norms, mobility, and labour demand as well as digital skills; the figure is an indicator, not proof of causation.

sources.title: Data For India: Access to phones and the internet (based on NSSO CAMS 2022-23)
GSMA: The Mobile Gender Gap Report 2024
Ideas for India: Digital gender divide amidst India's service-led growth
PRS Legislative Research: Cyber Crimes and Cyber Security of Women (Committee on the Empowerment of Women, 2026)

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and treat each claim as separate from solution chapters.
Preserve the evidence-cautious, non-preachy tone; do not shame individuals or imply all screen time is harmful.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not invent or generalise beyond the public evidence.
Flag any India figure that is a global benchmark or regional estimate so readers do not mistake it for a national measurement.
Point users to the Diagnosis and Designing for Substance chapters for platform-incentive and intervention context.

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.
